items.headline: Auto Sales Use Proposed for El Camino Real Commercial Site
items.summary: The Planning Commission is considering a special development permit to allow auto sales at 975 W. El Camino Real, an existing commercial property in the ECR-C zone. The project involves site modifications including repaving, restriping, and landscape upgrades, but no building expansion or exterior changes.

items.headline: 195-unit apartment complex permitted near Berryessa transit station
items.summary: A $32 million multi-family development at 825 Berryessa Station Way receives building permit, adding significant housing near the Berryessa BART station. The project represents one of the larger residential developments in the recent permit batch and aligns with the city's transit-oriented development goals.

items.headline: Water bill assistance pilot program launches for San José customers
items.summary: The city council approved a financial assistance pilot program to help water customers pay their bills. The program aims to support residents struggling with water service costs.

items.headline: Planning Commission considers 27-unit townhome project with affordable units
items.summary: The Planning Commission reviewed a residential project proposing 27 three-story townhomes, including 5 affordable units. The project represents a significant residential development in Cupertino that combines market-rate and affordable housing.

items.headline: Real estate lawsuit filed against city and SummerHill Homes
items.summary: A lawsuit (Fantozzi v. City of Cupertino and SummerHill Homes, Case No. 26CV492787) is ongoing; the city council discussed the case in closed session on May 11 and provided an open session report on the outcome.

items.headline: Council to hear 72-unit affordable housing project on Lytton Avenue
items.summary: Palo Alto is considering a six-story, 100% affordable housing development with 72 units on a 0.43-acre city-owned parking lot (Lot T) at 450 Lytton Avenue. The project requests state density bonus concessions and waivers to replace the existing 52-space surface lot.

items.headline: City Council adopts policy restricting use of municipal property
items.summary: Campbell City Council adopted a resolution establishing a policy that prohibits the use of city-owned and city-operated property for federal civil enforcement. The policy restricts how local government facilities can be made available to federal authorities.

items.headline: City declares hazardous brush and vegetation a public nuisance
items.summary: Los Gatos adopted a resolution formally declaring hazardous vegetation and brush a public nuisance, which enables the city to enforce removal and cite property owners. This gives the city legal authority to address overgrown vegetation that poses fire risk or safety hazards on private and public land.
